title: Deep dives could enhance freshwater production

Reverse osmosis membrane elements. A deeper understanding of how these membranes work could help improve global water security. Credit: Toray Industries, Inc.As climate change and population growth strain natural, freshwater resources, technological solutions to global water scarcity are becoming more important. A growing number of urban centres are using reverse osmosis — an energy-efficient technology for turning seawater or wastewater into drinking water — to meet basic water needs.“As populations grow and urbanization continues, reverse osmosis is one of the most practical and widely used solutions to address water scarcity,” argues Harutoki Shimura, a chemist from Toray Industries, a materials and chemical manufacturer based in Shiga, Japan.Today, the largest market for seawater desalination membranes is the Middle East, where reverse osmosis plays a crucial role in securing drinking water supplies. Shimura is General Manager at Toray Membrane Middle East (TMME) in Saudi Arabia, where he works to demonstrate Toray’s membrane technologies under real operating conditions in the region.But more people might be able to access secure water supplies if scientists had a deeper understanding of how water molecules pass through the porous, salt-rejecting membranes at the heart of reverse osmosis.With that goal in mind, a multidisciplinary team of researchers from Toray Industries — and Toray Research Center, an independent Toray Group company that conducts advanced contract analysis for both Toray and external companies — has been investigating reverse osmosis membranes.The researchers believe they have made findings that could finally decouple the trade-off between efficiency and purity in freshwater production by reverse osmosis.Harutoki Shimura at Toray Membrane Middle East in Saudi Arabia. Credit: Toray Industries, Inc.First-generation desalination plants consumed vast amounts of heat to evaporate and collect freshwater from salty seawater. In contrast, reverse osmosis membranes sieve out the salt. Pressurized seawater is forced through a nanoporous selectively permeable membrane that blocks the passage of salts and other contaminants.Reverse osmosis has been around for decades: it was first used commercially in the 1980s to produce ultrapure water for semiconductor manufacturing, before being adopted for seawater desalination in the 1990s.However, fundamental questions remain over the reverse osmosis membrane’s nanopore structure and the way water molecules slip through it. “Reverse osmosis is often now seen as a mature technology,” says Shimura. “But in reality, there’s significant room for further development.”Peering into nanoporesModern reverse osmosis membranes typically consist of a tough polyethylene terephthalate (PET) base, a polysulfone support and a crosslinked aromatic polyamide surface. The sub-0.2-micrometre-thick nanoporous polyamide is where salt rejection and selective water permeation occurs.Key to improving the process’ performance is analysing the way water molecules pass through the polyamide, says Shinichi Minegishi, general manager of Toray’s Global Environment Research Laboratories in Shiga, Japan. “The separation and water-transport mechanisms in these membranes have long been regarded as a black box,” he says. “Understanding these phenomena is essential for improving reverse osmosis membranes.”Toray researchers have spent decades deepening their understanding of reverse-osmosis water science. Previously, a Toray team used scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) to show that thinner polyamide layers, with a bumpy nanostructured surface that increased the functional layer’s surface area, exhibited significantly enhanced water permeability1.“By visualizing the membrane surface structure and highlighting the importance of expanded surface area, we developed membranes with 30% lower energy consumption,” says Minegishi.Toray researchers have now zoomed in even closer, to probe the polyamide membrane’s water-filled pores. “From the membrane perspective, there’s a fundamental trade-off between water permeability and separation performance,” explains Shimura. Polyamides with larger pores permit easier passage of water molecules and reduce energy consumption, but at the expense of more salts and other contaminants crossing the membrane.Shinichi Minegishi (at left) and Masaru Nakada at Toray’s Global Environment Research Laboratories in Shiga, Japan. Credit: Toray Industries, Inc.The team hopes to design membranes that enable water to slide through more easily without compromising salt rejection. “Understanding how water moves through the membrane, and where the resistance arises, is therefore essential,” says Shimura.The challenge is that no analytical technique can directly track water molecules flowing through a reverse-osmosis membrane. “Most conventional analytical techniques require dry conditions and cannot analyse structures as small as the sub-nanometre scale pores in the membrane,” says Shimura. The pores are too small for direct STEM analysis, for example.Synchrotron scienceTo circumvent this obstacle, Toray Industries membrane researchers leveraged their decades-long collaborative water-science partnership with analytical scientists at Toray Research Center.Scientists from the two organizations formed a fully integrated team, says Masaru Nakada from Toray Research Center. “Rather than simply performing measurements, we worked together from hypothesis formulation, analytical design and condition optimization through to data interpretation,” he says.The team designed a way to probe key interactions between water and the polyamide pores. They did this by combining multiple analytical techniques, not typically applied to commercial reverse-osmosis analysis, to study the system from different perspectives and build a picture of the phenomena at play.“We used neutron scattering to evaluate the mobility and diffusion behaviour of water molecules inside the polyamide membrane,” says Nakada. “We then used synchrotron-based X-ray emission spectroscopy to analyse the interaction between water and polyamide.”The analysis showed that water’s behaviour within the polyamide layer is much more complex than had been expected, Shimura says. “What surprised us the most was that, rather than existing in uniform state like water molecules in a gel, different states of water coexist within the membrane,” he says.Neutron-scattering analysis showed that some water molecules were strongly bound to the polyamide, while others remained unbound and free flowing2. The team then developed a liquid-flow-cell measurement technique that enabled them to study the hydrated membrane using the powerful X-ray beams generated at Japan’s SPring-8 synchrotron accelerator. The team found that the network of hydrogen bonds between water molecules in the membrane was partially distorted compared to the bonding network in free water3.Flow-on benefitsBased on these findings, and the team’s prior knowledge of reverse osmosis membranes, the researchers proposed a membrane-structure model in which water clusters formed in relatively larger pores are connected by narrow channels.These narrowings act as pinch points that squeeze water’s hydrogen-bonding network.Factoring these findings into next-generation membrane design will be the key to breaking the permeability-selectivity trade-off, Shimura thinks.“This research has clarified how we think about reverse osmosis membrane performance, starting from how water exists and moves inside the membrane,” he says. “We can now start to break down the factors behind the membrane’s water permeation and separation performance.”The potential energy savings from these next-generation membranes — and the implications for addressing global water scarcity — could be significant, Minegishi believes.“By more precisely controlling the reverse osmosis membrane molecular structure, we could significantly improve energy efficiency of desalination by increasing permeability while maintaining salt rejection,” he says.These advances will be further demonstrated through pilot-scale validation at the Middle East Water Treatment Technical Center (MEWTEC), where Toray is working to translate membrane science into practical desalination solutions for the world’s most water-stressed regions.
